policy_statement: Upgrade cogeneration at wastewater treatment plants to produce 9.6 megawatts of electricity from biogas.
mechanism_description: Upgrading wastewater treatment plant cogeneration increases electricity production from biogas, capturing methane and displacing fossil-based power generation.
primary_causal_pathway: Waste decomposition / wastewater treatment → ↑ methane capture + methane combustion/use → ↓ CH₄ emissions (and ↓ fossil generation) → ↓ net GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Install/upgrade landfill gas collection wells, piping, blowers, and gas treatment; or capture biogas at wastewater plants; route the gas to engines/turbines/boilers for combined heat and power (CHP) or electricity export, sometimes using recovered heat on-site.  
2) Mediating physical process: Anaerobic decomposition produces CH₄; collection systems intercept CH₄ that would otherwise vent/fugitive-emit; controlled combustion converts CH₄ to CO₂ (lower climate impact per unit carbon) and produces useful energy; generated energy can displace grid electricity/thermal energy that would otherwise come from fossil fuels.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in CO₂e emissions from (a) avoided methane emissions and (b) avoided fossil CO₂ from displaced generation (net of any project energy use/leakage).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is avoided GHG emissions through methane capture and utilization (plus fossil energy displacement).
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism acts through capturing methane and using it for energy, which directly lowers net GHG emissions; this is a classic mitigation pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although it can improve operational resilience/energy self-sufficiency (adaptation co-benefit) and can be framed as “resource recovery,” the largest climate effect is emissions reduction (CH₄ avoidance + fossil displacement), not reduced exposure to hazards or general efficiency absent emissions impacts.  
3) Edge cases applied: If gas is merely flared, mitigation still occurs via methane destruction; if energy displacement is minimal (e.g., off-grid with already-renewable supply), methane avoidance remains the dominant mitigation effect. If capture rates are poor or leakage is high, benefits can be reduced, but the intended and typical dominant pathway remains mitigation.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy defined by “waste-to-energy cogeneration with methane capture” necessarily targets methane destruction/utilization and therefore primarily delivers mitigation via reduced CO₂e.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether a specific policy instance should be tagged additionally as Resource Efficiency (energy recovery), Adaptation (critical facility power), or circular economy is policy- and context-dependent and requires policy-level evidence beyond the shared mechanism.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some representative examples reference wastewater plant cogeneration; if the feedstock is primarily digester biogas rather than landfill gas, the same mitigation logic applies. Net mitigation can be sensitive to methane leakage, baseline grid carbon intensity, and whether the project induces additional waste landfilling (which could offset benefits); those are implementation-specific and do not change the mechanism’s primary classification.
key_indicators: "Upgrade cogeneration"; "wastewater treatment plants"; "produce 9.6 megawatts of electricity"; "from biogas"
co_benefits: Potential air-quality improvements (reduced uncontrolled methane/odors), operational energy cost savings for the plants, improved energy resilience from on-site generation.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be framed as a target-setting item because of the "9.6 megawatts" figure, but the operative verb "Upgrade cogeneration" indicates an implementation/capex measure rather than a standalone target.
